data.title: Cream Rises To The Top: 2 Top-Tier BDCs To Buy
data.description:
As BDC investors, we are mostly yield-chasers, who want to both enhance and diversify our portfolio current income streams. The emphasis on yield per definition means that we fall more on the category of value-investors rather than those who are focused on significant price gains like the Mag-7 / AI-driven world.

And if we unpack what value investor really means is that basically it is all about buying assets or securities at prices that are below their intrinsic values - i.e., bargain hunting. Also the yield aspect comes with that as the lower the multiple is for any security, usually the higher the yield gets (provided that payout profiles remain unchanged).

At the same time, I hope that you will agree with me, most BDC investors are here for durable income extraction. In other words, it is not only about yield maximization and portfolio income diversification, but also about being able to sleep well at night. At least for me, the objective to get as high yield as possible, while not sacrificing the sustainability factor. Granted, sometime I make slight deviations by opening tactical positions in higher risk / higher return cases, say, CION Investment Corporation (CION) and Crescent Capital BDC, Inc. (CCAP), but all of that is very limited and still underpinned by robust assessments.

However, there is an inherent conflict in this approach: yield maximization vs. quality and thus valuations. Most of my BDC bull picks are concentrated in the discounted area of BDC world as there simply higher yielding opportunities lie and, in my view, there are also better working ground for hitting the balance between yield and safety.

Consequently, I have underemphasized players that trade at meaningful premiums, especially those that trade at significantly richer valuations than their NAV (not just on a relative basis to peers).

Main Street Capital (MAIN) is probably the best example - the most expensive BDC, but one that has managed to produce extraordinary returns despite, at some point, trading 2 times NAV:

While I see how sometimes it might be quite counterintuitive for value and high-yield investors to put "buy below intrinsic value" mantra aside, I think that it would be wrong to ignore the above P/NAV BDC category all together.

Just as there is a merit of introducing a separate - tactical BDC trade - bucket to access outsized return potential, there is for sure a value of paying extra to enhance the long-term income sustainability factor.

Let me flesh out this though through the following 2 BDCs.

2 BDCs that are worth the price

First of all, we have to understand that BDCs that trade at premium to NAV are not that many: eight out of 50+ players. And if we zoom into these eight BDCs, then we will notice a huge valuation discrepancy between the top and bottom level. The one that are more skewed to the upside in terms of P/NAV multiple levels are internally managed. Those, which are not internally managed, but trade at premium to NAV have generally robust non-accrual statistics, top-tier earnings quality metrics and, generally, sustainable capital allocation frameworks.

But it is exactly the valuation-rich BDCs - internally managed ones - that tend to be bypassed by value-oriented investors due to the issue I described above - i.e., relative premium making bargain hunters anxious about paying for something more than what it is worth.

I will elaborate on two concrete examples that showcase why such kind of approach might be suboptimal. These examples are Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) and Trinity Capital (TRIN).

Both of them are internally managed BDCs, which means that they do not have to incur neither fixed management fees (typically 1.5% of total book) nor incentive fees, which are around 15% that apply on a certain surplus income that exceeds the pre-defined hurdle rate (around 8%). It depends on BDC, but typically the total external management fees account for 15% - 20% of the total income.

So, because internally managed BDCs such as MAIN and TRIN do not have to absorb this cost item and apart from it all of the value-creation elements remain the same as for externally managed BDCs, it is only logical that the market arrives at relative premium valuations.

Furthermore, we have to also appreciate the alignment of interest aspect, where for internally managed BDCs the management team operate as direct employees and have stock option programs in place, which tend to be positive for long-term value creation.

And here is the thing: the presence of premiums over NAV allows MAIN and TRIN to access equity-financing at attractive terms. When the sector average indicates largely exhausted debt capacities and 100% base dividend coverage level, having an access to an accretive equity source is a major advantage.

It comes in extremely handy in the prevailing environment, when the M&A markets are opening up that offer an opportunity for BDCs to expand their loan books, which is basically one of the main tools to combat interest rate driven headwinds - i.e., offsetting negative price momentum with positive volume momentum so that the net effect is neutral or at least not that negative.

Apart from the internal management feature, there are multiple other drivers, which render these two BDCs decent buys.

For TRIN it is primarily about enjoying the industry-leading NII per share sensitivity to changes in base rates or SOFR. For example, a 100-basis point drop would lead to only 1% of a drop in the NII per share. This is so because of the embedded loan investment interest rate floors. On top of that, TRIN has a robust NII quality in terms of immaterial PIK. As of Q3, 2025, the PIK accounted for less than 1% of the total NII generation. The same applies to TRIN's non-accrual statistic. So, by acquiring TRIN, we are opening an exposure to high-quality BDC that carries an earnings profile, which is de-risked from unfavorable PIK and SOFR headwinds. And the best in all of this is that TRIN produces a monthly 12.9% dividend. Not bad deal at all.

MAIN's offer is a bit different and even more conservative (sustainable) than for TRIN. We can see that through ~7% yielding monthly dividend and much higher multiple - i.e., 1.90x vs. 1.19x (for MAIN and TRIN, respectively).

In MAIN's case we get a BDC, which has the fourth most conservative balance sheet in the entire space. The debt to equity is 0.73x, providing a solid basis for debt-driven growth as well, which is obviously even more accretive than funding that with equity (even though it can be sourced cheaply). In its portfolio MAIN has close to 10% high-quality equity exposure, where the lion's share of this book consists of cash-flowing and dividend producing assets. These are exactly the right targets to bring to the market for monetization, when the M&A and interest rate environments are so favorable. The benefit here is that equity-driven returns are inherently negatively correlated to interest rate changes as opposed to plain vanilla first lien loan investments. Finally, MAIN is a lower middle market BDC, where the spread compression has not been as pronounced battle as for the rest of the space due to the inherent constraints for large money managers to justify their time spend for small ticket size transactions (e.g., to move the needle for billion dollar portfolios, investing in $5 - $15 million loans is not that efficient, to say the least).

All in all, sometimes it is worth paying premium to get better quality. TRIN and MAIN are two such examples. Maybe in the short to medium-term they could underperform the index due to lack of discounted valuations, but in the long-run, which is what matters for me, the performance should be solid.

data.title: Market Premium For 10-Year Yield Holds Near Fair Value Estimate
data.description:
The market premium for the US 10-year Treasury yield continues to trade near a fair value estimate, based on the average estimate of three models run by CapitalSpectator.com. Today’s update shows an ongoing extension of the near-neutral level for the benchmark rate in recent months, following several years of a high market premium.

The current estimate indicates that the 10-year yield traded roughly 22 basis points over fair value in December, based on analytics using monthly data. That’s close to the lowest market premium in three years.

The recent shift toward a neutral level continues to highlight the reversal of the previous sharp rise in the market premium during 2022-2024. The recent drop from the previous peak reminds us that valuation for the 10-year yield remains cyclical, albeit on an irregular and difficult-to-forecast timing basis.

Despite the seemingly random fluctuations in the market premium/discount through time, history suggests that the cycle revolves around zero (neutral). In turn, this dynamic provides an implied forecast for managing exposure to maturity risk for a bond-market strategy.

The current reading, which reflects a slight premium, implies a recommendation to adopt a more or less neutral stance for maturity risk vis-à-vis the 10-year yield.

By contrast, a higher allocation to maturity risk looked timely in late 2023, when the market premium was relatively high – above 120 basis points at the peak. Since then, the 10-year yield has fallen, and the bond market has rallied.

There’s no guarantee that the cycle will continue to repeat, although it does appear to rhyme. Betting otherwise still looks like an excessively risky assumption.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Disinflation Gains A Second Wind, As Forecast
data.description:
A disappointing earnings report from a bellwether in the banking sector set the tone for yesterday’s trading. JPMorgan stock slipped 4% after mixed results due to lower-than-expected investment banking fees. Financials were further pressured by President Trump’s vocal support for legislation to curb swipe fees charged by credit card issuers. Small-cap stocks bucked the trend in reaction to a better-than-expected Consumer Price Index (CPI) report for December. That combination left the major market indexes relatively flat with the exception of the Dow Jones Industrial Average, which was weighed down by its financial exposure.

The headline inflation number rose 0.3% for the month of December and 2.7% over the past year, in line with expectations, while the core rate rose a less-than-expected 0.2%, which resulted in the annualized rate holding steady at a four-year low of 2.6%. It was expected to increase to 2.8%. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, shelter costs rose 0.4% for the month and 3.2% over the past year, serving as the most significant factor in overall inflation. When we exclude shelter, the core CPI rose just 0.1% in December.

After a tariff-related spike last year, it appears that disinflation has gained a second wind and reasserted itself, as businesses absorbed most of the import costs rather than passing them on to consumers. There are some exceptions, with food prices being a sore spot for the American consumer. Food prices at home and away from home rose 0.7% last month. There were also some oddities in the report, such as the 3.3% drop in costs for wireless telephone services last month, which shaved 8 basis points off the core number. I know my Verizon bill didn’t go down 3.3% last month. I won’t quibble about the accuracy of the report. My interest is in how the market will respond and how consumers will react.

The bottom line is that the rate of overall price increases appears to be waning again, and even if there are inaccuracies in some line items, which I would not doubt, the most important one being shelter costs are still overstated in my view. This is primarily due to the way the BLS calculates the number, as I have iterated over the past several years. Rents are not rising at an annualized rate of 3%, as the report purports. Home prices are not rising at that rate either.

According to Apartment List, rents in December declined 0.8%, which is seasonal, while the more important year-on-year drop in rental rates was 1.3%. That is deflation, and its driver has been a surge in multifamily construction, with more than 600k new units on the market in 2024 and nearly half that number in the first half of last year. Immigration policy is also starting to have an impact with fewer renters in the market.

The Case-Shiller Home Price Index rose 1.3% in October, which was the lowest rate of price increase since July 2023, and I seriously doubt that rate is increasing given market dynamics in December.

A major criticism of the government’s calculation is that it does not account for homeowners’ insurance or property taxes, which have been on the rise, but I don’t think either offset the deflation we are seeing in rents and the negligible increase in home prices that exists today.

From the CPI report, we can derive inflation-adjusted (real) weekly take-home pay for workers, which increased 1.1% over the prior year in December. That is the same rate of increase we saw in December 2024. This is vitally important to the rate of consumer spending growth, which accounts for two-thirds of our overall rate of economic growth. Real income growth is the lifeblood of real consumer spending growth, which is what fuels our economic growth.

data.title: From Turbulence To Takeoff: Why Delta Could Become A Solid Dividend Grower
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to classic dividend growth stocks, Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL) doesn't necessarily come to the mind of investors. However, 2026 and beyond could see the airline giant become a solid dividend grower to give your portfolio a liftoff.

DAL currently appears cheap with a forward multiple of less than 10x. With interest rates likely to continue declining and strategic growth initiatives, DAL may be worth considering if you're a long-term investor.

In this article, I discuss Delta's latest earnings, growth outlook, and why this dividend stock could become a solid dividend grower for the foreseeable future.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Delta Air Lines, assigning the stock a Buy rating back in June. Despite turbulence, DAL has performed exceptionally well, up over 44% in the past 7 months.

They also surpassed my price target of $70.62 before the recent pullback. The airline giant significantly outperformed the S&P (SP500) over the same period, up 16%.

During their Q1 earnings, DAL managed to beat analysts' estimates on both their top and bottom lines. EPS amounted to $0.46, a beat by $0.07. Revenue beat by $580 million.

Despite this, DAL's headwinds were apparent as EPS remained flat from the prior year. Revenue did see some slight growth, up 3.3% to $13 billion.

Free cash flow was down from $1.4 billion to $1.3 billion. The company also announced a 25% dividend increase and a $1 billion buyback program, a testament to their improving fundamentals.

Solid Quarter To Close Out The Fiscal Year

Delta Air Lines reported their Q4 earnings to close out the year with mixed results. EPS was in line with analysts' estimates at $1.55, while revenue posted a solid beat by $310 million.

This amounted to $16 billion, a growth rate of 11.1% year-over-year. For the full year, revenue increased 2.3% from $57 billion to $58.3 billion, solid considering the tough economic backdrop. Top-line growth was a result of premium revenue increasing 7% and cargo revenue growing 9%.

DAL's performance should be commended due to volatile fuel prices, financially-constrained consumers as a result of rises in unemployment, historically high interest rates, and residual impacts from the government shutdown.

You can see from the chart below, Delta's revenue per seat available and per passenger declined slightly from the year prior. And yet total revenue increased.

Earnings per share declined year-over-year, down 5.5% from $6.16 in 2024 to $5.82. This was a result of lower pre-tax profits, which fell year-over-year from $1.6 billion to $1.3 billion.

Operating margins also fell from 12% to 10%. And while I expect near-term volatility for DAL, the company's fundamental improvements set the company up for solid growth in 2026 and beyond.

2026 Guidance

During the quarter, management issued guidance for 2026, with EPS expected in a range of $6.50-$7.50. At midpoint, this represents a growth rate of 20%, a complete turnaround from FY25.

Free cash flow is expected in a range of $3 billion to $4 billion, lower than 2025 due to increased CAPEX of $5.5 billion. Leverage is expected to continue trending downward, all positive signs going forward.

With interest rates expected to continue falling and a likelihood of seeing cost reductions, I anticipate management raising guidance as the quarters go on, resulting in a solid growth setup for 2026.

Growth Drivers

As previously mentioned, lower base rates should provide relief for DAL and its peers. Moreover, CPI cooled slightly ahead of expectations, showing that inflation seems to be under control despite tariffs.

The airline giant also recently announced a multi-year partnership with Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) to allow guests access to the Delta SKY360 Club lounge during events.

This makes DAL the official airline partner for SPHR. And as a result, DAL will receive promotion during events.

This should result in solid promotion and growth going forward, particularly with its rewards members.

They have also been leveraging artificial intelligence with the launch of Delta Concierge, an AI-powered assistant in the Delta app to assist and deliver support to airline members.

And lastly, the company continues to expand its fleet and opened a new Delta Sky Club, their second largest, in Salt Lake City, Utah.

Why DAL Could Become A Solid Dividend Grower

Although airlines are highly cyclical and considered riskier, Delta's fundamental setup appears poised to deliver solid dividend growth. As mentioned earlier, the company announced a $1 billion share buyback program in Q1.

For the fiscal year, management didn't repurchase any shares as they continued to prioritize paying down debt, a better option in my opinion. The current buyback program is for 3 years, and I expect management to opportunistically buy back shares during this time frame.

As a result of this and their attractive forward P/E, I expect management at DAL could see solid earnings growth over the next 3 to 5 years.

DAL re-initiated their dividend in 2023 and last raised it a solid 25%. And their dividend was well-covered with only $440 million in dividends paid for the entire year.

DAL also saw record free cash flow for the year with $4.6 billion, surpassing guidance set in Q4 of last year. Peer United Airlines (UAL) expects to generate $3 billion in FCF this fiscal year.

Management expected FCF in a range of $4 billion. This gives DAL a safe payout of just 9.6% and ample room for double-digit dividend growth going forward.

Moreover, I suspect solid, double-digit increases in the foreseeable future, which should drive share price growth as a result.

Improved Balance Sheet

Delta Air Lines also improved their balance sheet, managing to pay down debt and decrease leverage to 2.4x. This was 2.6x in Q1. And as previously mentioned, management is targeting 2.0x by the end of 2026 and a long-term target of 1.0x.

This was higher than peer Southwest Airlines (LUV), whose leverage ratio in their latest quarter was 2.1x.

Delta's liquidity was robust, with $7.4 billion, including $3.1 billion in revolver capacity. Net debt declined $3.7 billion from the prior year to $14 billion.

Total debt stood at $22 billion. Their strong cash flow generation also gives the company flexibility to continue deleveraging.

Attractive Valuation At Less Than 10x Earnings

At the current price, DAL's forward P/E sits at 9.81x, relatively low for a company of their caliber. Although airline stocks are considered riskier and highly cyclical, I believe the stock deserves to trade higher.

Using United Airlines' guidance midpoint of $10, they currently have a slightly higher P/E of 11.4x. Over the next 2 to 4 years, I suspect DAL can at least trade in the 12x-14x range despite this being above their 5-year average of 9.45x.

Because of COVID and the high inflationary environment over the past 5 or so years, I don't think DAL's 5-year average should be viewed as a reliable benchmark.

Furthermore, buybacks and partnerships could fuel growth, resulting in solid price appreciation. Additionally, lower interest rates should provide tailwinds. And together, I see solid upside over the long-term despite near- to medium-term volatility. Using approximately a 13x multiple, DAL offers strong upside over the next two years of 57%.

Risks & Conclusion

Despite CPI cooling, which should provide relief going forward, labor market uncertainty and geopolitical issues still pose risks. Moreover, airline stocks are highly cyclical, likely the reason they trade at low multiples.

If we see further labor market weakening, perhaps due to AI impacts, I expect DAL could continue seeing declining revenue per passenger and seat impact margins.

However, long-term, I like where Delta Air Lines is headed, as their improved balance sheet, robust cash flows, and a seemingly improving economy set them up for success in 2026 and beyond.

As a result, I maintain my Buy rating on DAL. Their well-covered dividend and low payout ratio could result in strong price appreciation for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Frontline: How Fleet Renewal, Venezuela Oil, And A 17% Yield Could Drive Returns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Frontline (FRO) before, where I outlined the company’s background in detail, explained why I considered it overvalued, and why I preferred Okeanis (ECO). Since the article was published, Frontline has returned 19%, while Okeanis has returned 47% over the same period. However, due to recent developments, I’m upgrading the stock from sell to buy. While I still prefer Okeanis, Frontline should benefit from the sector sentiment.

Background

Frontline is the largest tanker pure-play, and after a recent transaction, its future fleet will consist of 81 vessels: 42 VLCCs, 21 Suezmax tankers, and 18 LR2/Aframax tankers.

This transaction is transformative as the company will sell its eight oldest VLCCs, which have an average age of around 10 years, and acquire nine newbuilds, most of them delivering in 2026, for just $32 million per vessel.

Moreover, back in October there were rumors that Frontline was planning to sell its LR2 fleet. While the sale didn’t materialize, those rumors have resurfaced recently, suggesting that the company is still trying to sell those vessels.

This will transform the company into the largest and with the newest fleet among peers, especially if LR2s are finally sold.

Another reason for optimism is the return of Venezuelan oil to the legitimate market. Until now, most of Venezuelan oil was exported using dark fleet tankers headed to China; however, from now on, as the US will control the oil, it will be transported using legitimate tankers. This is highly relevant, as this is not just the reopening of a new route but the revival of a completely new market that was lost years ago. It represents the creation of new ton-miles for the legitimate fleet.

Frontline’s CEO, Lars Barstad, summarized it really well in a tweet:

Moreover, Venezuelan oil requires diluents for transportation. Until now, these were sourced from Russia or Iran in their dark fleet. However, now those tankers will no longer be used. Instead, diluents will come from the US with compliant tankers, and Vitol has already sent the first one.

As the tweet states, another potentially even bigger catalyst could be if Iranian oil returns to the legitimate fleet; however, despite the recent unrest, I’m not optimistic the Iranian regime could be overthrown.

Frontline is the best-positioned tanker stock to benefit from those developments, as it is the most liquid and the go-to stock when such events unfold, as it is the most known among retail and fund investors.

This favorable treatment is in part due to its generous dividend policy of paying quarterly dividends equal to or close to EPS, which translates into strong dividends and yields when rates soar.

Financial Position and Valuation

Frontline has a comfortable financial position with $189 million in cash and $3.2 billion in debt. After the announced fleet renewal transaction, the company will receive about $830 million during this quarter and will spend $1.2 billion on the new vessels; most of it will be paid towards delivery of each vessel from Q3 2026. This means that the debt will increase; however, the company will have one more ship and a much younger fleet.

While the company provided attractive guidance, it should be noted that this is based on load to discharge, which overstates the numbers. Despite this, and considering the rates evolution, Q4 will be the best quarter in the company’s recent history, as I expect an EPS of around $1.15 and a similar dividend. This implies a yield above 17%. Moreover, after a brief collapse in rates at year end, rates continue to be really strong, which ensures another great quarter in Q1.

Regarding the valuation, the data points from the transaction suggest that the company is not trading as expensively as it could seem, due to vessel prices rising quickly:

However, asset prices are at historical levels, and to be sustainable, rates have to remain strong on average. For the stock to continue to rise, it needs positive sentiment in the sector and good rates to start trading in relation to its yield more than its NAV.

I believe that the company could reach $30 per share (around 1.4x NAV) if momentum continues.

Risks

The elevated valuation carries risks, as huge expectations are priced in. If those expectations do not materialize, the stock will suffer.

Starting a position at current levels, beyond a trade while momentum supports it, requires elevated rates (not necessarily at current levels, but higher than historical levels). This is especially true when rates are entering the softest months of the year (Q2 and especially Q3).

Asset prices should remain elevated too, which requires high rates to justify current valuations.

Another important risk, related to the above, is if OPEC+ decides to slash output again. In that case, tanker demand will be reduced, and tanker rates will follow. For me, this is the most important risk.

Finally, a short-term risk is that the company will sell those vessels in Q1 and won’t receive any until Q3; this will substantially reduce the dividend capacity in Q2 and Q3.

Conclusion

Frontline has announced a transformative fleet renewal that will position the company as the largest and youngest public tanker fleet owner.

This, combined with positive momentum from the Venezuela news, leads me to upgrade the stock to a buy. However, to justify higher valuations, rates must continue to remain elevated in the future, and the stock should start trading more based on its yield rather than asset values.

data.title: Urgent Warning: These REITs Face High Risk Of Dividend Cuts
data.description:
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) recently cut its dividend, and unfortunately for us, we did not see that one coming.

It was, of course, well-known that the life science property sector was oversupplied, but we thought that the REIT's long lease terms would limit the damage of this cyclical downturn. The REIT also had one of the strongest balance sheets in the entire REIT sector and a low payout ratio based on FFO.

But then the new administration came in and caused unprecedented policy chaos, which essentially killed the funding of this sector. No one is going to invest billions into the research and development of new drugs if the potential payoff on such investments is highly uncertain due to the constant threats of tariffs, pricing restrictions, new stricter regulations, and more.

As a result, the demand for these properties crashed, even as the sector was already oversupplied, making an already bad situation a lot worse, pushing ARE to cut its dividend in an abundance of caution.

This caught us off guard, and we paid the price:

This is a good example of what typically happens as a REIT cuts its dividend. Income investors and dividend-oriented funds indiscriminately sell the stock based on the change in capital allocation policy, regardless of the rest of the story.

The new imbalance in supply and demand for the stock then causes it to crash to much lower levels.

That is why it is generally preferable to avoid holding REITs that are at high risk of cutting their dividend.

Fortunately, we have been quite successful at that over the years, predicting the dividend cuts of many REITs, including:

  • Global Net Lease (GNL)
  • Easterly Government Properties (DEA)
  • Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT)
  • Service Properties Trust (SVC)
  • And many others...

Today, we are going to give you our latest dividend cut predictions.

Be very cautious about these three REITs:

KKR Real Estate Finance (KREF)

KKR Real Estate Finance (KREF) is a popular mortgage REIT.

It is popular because it is offering a 12.7% dividend yield, which many seem to think is sustainable, and the REIT is managed by the private equity powerhouse KKR (KKR).

The REIT already cut its dividend significantly in early 2024, and this appears to have led many to believe that the current dividend payout should now be sustainable. The management's own commentary on the earnings call also seems rather optimistic. They said this on the Q3 call:

"We set our dividend at a level in which we believe we can cover distributable earnings prior to realized losses over the long term."

An analyst even asked them about potentially hiking the dividend, which makes it seem as if the market is still sleeping on the risk of another cut.

But I think that investors should be concerned.

The current dividend is not covered by distributable cash flow, and the REIT is heavily leveraged with a debt-to-equity of about 4x.

The management remains optimistic because they think that they can sell some of their repossessed properties and reinvest the proceeds into new loans, which will then lead to a surge in distributable cash flow.

I hope for them that it works out well. But when you are so heavily leveraged, I think that everything needs to be executed to perfection for the dividend to be maintained.

And the big elephant in the room here is that the REIT's loan portfolio is today 14% invested in the life science sector. As we discussed in the introduction of this article, Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) is currently facing severe headwinds, and those same headwinds also apply to KREF.

In fact, KREF may face even greater headwinds given that its life science collateral is inferior in quality relative to the portfolio of Alexandria Real Estate on average.

In the last quarter, KREF had to downgrade the risk rating of a life science property, and I expect more of that in 2026.

The issue is that when you have so much leverage, each loss will result in a big step back, and this is what I think will ultimately lead to another dividend cut.

The main risk to the thesis is that KREF manages to rapidly sell some of its recently foreclosed assets and redeploys the proceeds accretively, leading to a recovery in dividend coverage. I think that this is unlikely.

One Liberty Properties (OLP)

One Liberty Properties (OLP) is a diversified net lease REIT focusing primarily on industrial properties.

A lot of investors hear "industrial" and immediately think "steady high growth" supported by e-commerce and onshoring.

But the industrial property sector is very versatile, and there exist vast differences in risk profiles and growth prospects.

A newly built e-commerce distribution center leased to Amazon (AMZN) may sell at a low 4% cap rate, even as an older manufacturing facility leased to a middle-market non-credit tenant may sell at a 9% cap rate.

Both belong to the industrial peer group, but their future prospects are day and night.

OLP is more heavily focused on those riskier industrial properties that sell at high cap rates.

That on its own does not mean that the dividend isn't sustainable.

But the problem is that these are clearly riskier, more cyclical assets (as you wouldn't get such high cap rates in the industrial property sector otherwise), and we may be approaching the end of the cycle.

We had nearly as many job layoffs in 2025 as in 2009, and the Fed is busy cutting rates because of this reason.

Even that would be fine if the REIT had a strong balance sheet and a low dividend payout ratio, but it is the opposite here.

The REIT has a high 6.4x Debt-to-EBITDA. Here is how its leverage compares to some of its net lease peers.

Debt-to-EBITDA
Agree Realty (ADC) 3.8x
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) 3.8x
One Liberty Properties (OLP) 6.4x

And its dividend payout ratio is also unusually high:

Payout Ratio
Agree Realty (ADC) 73%
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) 62%
One Liberty Properties (OLP) 100%

The REIT only has 4.5 years left on its leases on average, and I predict that as more leases reach expiration, the REIT will deal with growing vacancies, rising capex, and releasing costs, which will ultimately lead to a dividend cut.

The main risk to OLP's dividend cut thesis is if interest rates rapidly return to lower levels, reducing the balance sheet stress, even as the economy remains stronger than expected, resulting in better than expected releasing prospects.

National Storage Affiliates (NSA)

National Storage Affiliates (NSA) is the smallest of the self-storage REITs. Its close peers include CubeSmart (CUBE), Public Storage (PSA), and ExtraSpace Storage (EXR).

When its cost of capital was low, NSA was able to grow the fastest of its peer group, as its small size and the large investment spreads resulted in stronger accretion from new acquisitions.

This also resulted in stronger total returns for most of its history, which made it very popular among many REIT investors.

But then self-storage became oversupplied, and since then, NSA has been the worst performer in its peer group due to its smaller size, higher leverage, and weaker assets on average.

Its share price has dropped much more than that of its peers, and as a result, it now trades at a historically high 7.3% dividend. The spread relative to its peers is much larger than usual:

And given that the REIT's AFFO payout ratio is already above 100% and the REIT has high leverage with a 7x Debt-to-EBITDA, I think that a cut is likely just around the corner. Possibly already over the coming quarters.

The main risk to this thesis is a faster-than-expected recovery of the self-storage sector, resulting in strong same-property NOI growth already in the near term. This could potentially save them from a dividend cut.

I am actually bullish on the long-term prospects of the self-storage property sector, and we hold a position in CubeSmart at High-Yield Landlord but would not dare to invest in NSA due to the high risk of a dividend cut.

It is by staying away from such situations that we have managed to outperform the market over the long run.

data.title: Oakmark International Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fourth-quarter highlights

Top contributors

Bayer (BAYZF)

DSV (DSDVF)

Samsung Electronics Pfd

Top detractors

Alibaba Group (BABA)

CNH Industrial (CNH)

Dassault Systemes (DASTY)

New purchases

AstraZeneca ADR (AZN)

IMCD NV (IMCDY)

Sanofi (SNY)

Symrise (SYIEF)

Unilever (UL)

Final sales

Exor (EXXRF)

Komatsu (KMTUY)

Mercedes-Benz Group (MBGAF)

Novartis (NVS)

SKF Cl B

Sandvik (SDVKY)

Schroders (SHNWF)

Worldline (WWLNF)

  • The Fund (Investor Class)(OAKIX) underperformed the benchmark, the MSCI World ex USA Index, for the quarter, but outperformed the benchmark for the calendar year and since inception.
  • At the sector level, health care and financials were the largest contributors to performance, while consumer staples was the only detractor from performance.
  • Geographically, the top three regional allocations are 68.1% in Europe ex U.K., 13.8% in the U.K. and 8.7% in the Asia ex Japan. For the quarter, Germany, Switzerland and France were top contributors to performance. China, the U.S. and Ireland were top detractors from performance. Emerging markets accounted for 10.2% of the portfolio.
  • Valuations across many international markets remain relatively attractive even after a year of strong returns. The opportunity set has shifted somewhat toward more stable, higher quality businesses and we are taking advantage of that opportunity.

Top Contributor | Detractor

Top contributor

Bayer was the top contributor during the quarter. Two anticipated events developed in the company’s favor. First, Bayer enjoyed a positive readout on the company’s stroke drug, Asundexian, which met its primary endpoint in a recent phase III trial. Asundexian has the potential to be a blockbuster and support a return to growth for the Pharmaceuticals business, in our opinion. Second, the United States Solicitor General recommended that the Supreme Court hear Bayer’s appeal in Durnell v. Monsanto, increasing the odds that the RoundUp matter is heard by the Court. Both events support our investment thesis for Bayer as management works to turn around fundamental performance and contain litigation risk.

Top detractor

Alibaba Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The China-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price declined as it reported mixed results. The core E-commerce business continues to perform well, and Cloud revenue growth is accelerating. However, the company’s earnings were negatively impacted due to significant spending on subsidies to grow their Quick Commerce business, in our opinion. We believe losses from Quick Commerce will be reduced over time and continue to believe the company is well-positioned for long-term growth, having been one of the early investors in Chinese AI. Over time, we think it can leverage its advanced capabilities and leading market position to unlock further value.

Portfolio Activity

New purchases

AstraZeneca is one of the largest pharmaceutical companies in the world. It researches, develops and commercializes prescription medicines designed to treat lung and breast cancers, cardiorenal diseases, respiratory problems and other rare diseases. We believe AstraZeneca's robust on-market portfolio and sector-leading late-stage pipeline provide an attractive growth profile. Moreover, we believe the company can build on its long track record of a productive research and development program, thanks to its innovative culture and exceptional management team. CEO Pascal Soriot is one of the industry’s best executives, and he has cultivated a deep bench of talent, a robust decision-making framework and a differentiated R&D culture that should drive strong growth for years to come, in our view. Recent concerns over United States regulations have overshadowed AstraZeneca’s merits and weighed on the broader pharmaceutical industry. This opened a window for us to purchase shares of this company at a price well below our estimate of its intrinsic value.

IMCD is a global leader in the marketing, sales and distribution of specialty chemicals and ingredients. The company is widely regarded as best-in-class, benefiting from a highly technical salesforce and extensive global scale. These strengths make IMCD a preferred partner, enabling it to drive consistent organic growth, enhance visibility across distribution channels, and streamline points of contact for customers and suppliers alike. IMCD specializes in the "true specialties" area of the market, where chemicals or ingredients often have a material impact on end-product performance. That translates into significant pricing power and demand inelasticity – market features we find particularly attractive. Despite strong fundamentals, the stock price has fallen significantly due to a combination of cyclical downturn and market fears that we believe are overstated. This presented us with the opportunity to invest in a well-managed company with substantial M&A opportunities that we think should re-rate over the long term.

Sanofi is a global pharmaceutical company developing biologics, vaccines and healthcare solutions to prevent and treat a wide range of conditions in immunology, hemophilia, rare diseases and general medicine. We think there is a lot to be excited about as management has prioritized innovation by aggressively investing in research and development to cultivate a pipeline of promising products. Despite strong fundamentals, the stock has been weighed down by a volatile year for the vaccine market, investor concerns about patent cliffs and minimal value ascription to its pipeline. This created the opportunity for us to invest in a company we believe is improving, with a long runway for future growth as vaccine markets normalize and new product potential is realized.

Symrise is a global leader in the development of flavors, fragrances, and specialty ingredients used across food, beverages and consumer products. We find the industry's consolidated structure attractive and value Symrise's leading positions in pet food palatability, beverage flavors and personal care ingredients that are well supported by its broad portfolio and long-standing customer relationships. While these ingredients represent a small portion of the total cost of goods sold, they are critical to the performance and differentiation of the end product, creating sticky long-term buyer-seller relationships. We also appreciate management's renewed focus on profitability and cash flow, with early signs of positive operational and financial inflection supporting improved results going forward. Despite this strong outlook, we believe that the stock has underperformed recently due to stalling organic growth under previous leadership, creating an attractive entry point for us to invest in a company poised for growth that is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of intrinsic value.

Unilever is a global consumer goods company that develops and markets everyday food and personal care brands for billions of consumers worldwide. Anchored by iconic brands such as Dove, Knorr, Hellmann's, and Vaseline, Unilever’s refreshed management team is driving improved execution and strategic discipline to deliver more consistent growth, with a focus on higher-margin categories. Specifically, we appreciate their undertaking of various self-help initiatives, including cost-savings programs and brand divestments, which we believe will help unlock sustained value in the future. Despite Unilever's strong outlook, it trades at a discount to its peer group as prior undermanagement has resulted in 1% volume growth for the past decade. This provided us the opportunity to invest in a strong company with leading brands and an improved management team that is poised to increase per-share value.

PORTFOLIO MANAGERS*

(Year joined Harris | Oakmark)

David G. Herro, CFA (1992)

Tony Coniaris, CFA (1999)

Eric Liu, CFA (2009)

*Effective July 1, 2025 Tony Coniaris replaced Michael Manelli as a portfolio manager of the Fund.

Expense ratios are as of the Fund’s most recent prospectus dated January 28, 2025, as amended and restated January 30, 2025, March 14, 2025 and May 19, 2025; actual expenses may vary. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Since inception returns for the indexes are calculated based on the Investor Class inception date. “Linked performance”: Advisor and Institutional Class shares commenced operations on 11/30/2016. The performance attributed to the those share classes prior to that date is that of the Investor Class shares from 9/30/1992-11/30/2016. Performance prior to 11/30/2016 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of Advisor and Institutional Class shares which would have had similar, but potentially higher returns due to lower expenses. R6 Class shares commenced operations on 12/15/2020. The performance attributed to the R6 Class shares prior to that date is that of the Investor Class shares from 9/30/1992-11/30/2016, and then the performance of the Institutional Class shares from 11/30/2016-12/15/2020. Performance prior to 12/15/2020 has not been adjusted to reflect the lower expenses of R6 Class shares. During this period, R6 Class shares would have had similar, but potentially higher returns due to lower expenses.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results. The performance data quoted represents past performance. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Total return includes change in share prices and, in each case, includes reinvestment of dividends and capital gain distributions. The investment return and principal value vary so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost.

The securities mentioned above comprise the following percentages of the Oakmark International Fund’s total net assets as of 12/31/2025: Alibaba Group 1.7%, AstraZeneca ADR 1.3%, Bayer 2.3%, CNH Industrial 2.2%, Dassault Systemes

2.3%, DSV 2.2%, Exor 0%, IMCD NV 1.0%, Komatsu 0%, Mercedes-Benz Group 0%, Novartis 0%, Samsung Electronics Pfd 1.8%, Sandvik 0%, Sanofi 1.3%, Schroders 0%, SKF Cl B 0%, Symrise 1.3%, Unilever 1.4% and Worldline 0%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change without notice and are not intended as recommendations of individual stocks.

Access the full list of holdings for the Oakmark International Fund here or visit www.oakmark.com.

The information, data, analyses, and opinions presented herein (including current investment themes, the portfolio managers’ research and investment process, and portfolio characteristics) are for informational purposes only and represent the investments and views of the portfolio managers

and Harris Associates L.P. as of the date written and are subject to change and may change based on market and other conditions without notice.

This content is not a recommendation of or an offer to buy or sell a security and is not warranted to be correct, complete, or accurate.

Certain comments herein are based on current expectations and are considered “forward-looking statements.” These forward-looking statements reflect assumptions and analyses made by the portfolio managers and Harris Associates L.P. based on their experience and perception of historical trends, current conditions, expected future developments, and other factors they believe are relevant. Actual future results are subject to a number of investment and other risks and may prove to be different from expectations. Readers are cautioned not to place undue reliance on the forward-looking statements.

The MSCI World ex USA Index (Net) is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure international developed market equity performance, excluding the U.S. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country. This benchmark calculates reinvested dividends net of withholding taxes. This index is unmanaged and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

The MSCI World ex USA Value Index (Net) represents returns for large- and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across 22 of 23 Developed Markets (excluding the United States). The value investment style characteristics for index construction are based on book value-to-price, 12-month forward earnings-to-price, and dividend yield. The Total Return Index (Net) includes reinvested dividends net of foreign withholding tax. This index is unmanaged, and investors cannot invest directly in this index.

On occasion, Harris may determine, based on its analysis of a particular multinational issuer, that a country classification different from MSCI best reflects the issuer’s country of investment risk. In these instances, reports with country weights and performance attribution will differ from reports using MSCI classifications. Harris uses its own country classifications in its reporting processes, and these classifications are reflected in the included materials.

Investing involves risk; principal loss is possible. There is no guarantee the Fund's investment objective will be achieved. Value stocks may fall out of favor with investors and underperform growth stocks during given periods. Foreign securities present risks that in some ways may be greater than U.S. investments. Those risks include: currency fluctuation; different regulation, accounting standards, trading practices and levels of available information; generally higher transaction costs; and political risks. The Fund’s portfolio tends to be invested in a relatively small number of stocks. As a result, the appreciation or depreciation of any one security held by the Fund will have a greater impact on the Fund’s net asset value than it would if the Fund invested in a larger number of securities. Although that strategy has the potential to generate attractive returns over time, it also increases the Fund’s volatility. These and other risk considerations are described in detail in the Fund’s prospectus.

All information provided is as of 12/31/2025 unless otherwise specified.

Before investing in any Oakmark Fund, you should carefully consider the Fund's investment objectives, risks, management fees and other expenses. This and other important information is contained in a Fund's prospectus and summary prospectus. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing. For more information, please visit Oakmark.com or call 1-800-OAKMARK (1-800- 625-6275).

data.title: The Dollar Consolidates While Japan Steps Up Its Intervention Threats And Decision Day For The SCOTUS
data.description:
The dollar is mostly softer, but the consolidative tone persists. The yen remains the notable exception. Japanese officials have taken several steps up the intervention ladder with heightened warnings. The market initially extended the greenback’s gains to JPY159.45 before taking some profits and pushing the dollar to around JPY158.60. The market senses it is on thin ice, but many look for an intervention-inspired drop to buy the dollar cheaper. Bond markets seem well-behaved in the face of a five-day, 11% rally in oil prices. The US 10-year yield frayed the 4.20% cap in recent days but is near a four-day low now, below 4.15%. The weak yen and higher JGB yields seem to go hand-in-hand, but it has not deterred Japanese equities from setting record highs.

The North American session features US PPI and retail sales and no fewer than five Fed officials. Also, today is a decision day for the Supreme Court. It does not pre-announce which cases have been decided, but it is possible that the ruling on the challenge to President Trump’s tariffs under emergency powers is handed down. A rejection, which is widely anticipated, is thought to be initially negative for bond and the dollar but positive for equities.

Prices

G10

• The euro’s inside day on Tuesday neutralizes the knee-jerk gain on the back of the latest challenge to the Federal Reserve’s independence. It is coiling inside yesterday’s range today and has been stuck between about $1.1635 and $1.1660. The session high was recorded in late European morning turnover. It looks to have stalled, giving a downside bias in early North American trading. Last Friday’s low, slightly below $1.1620, needs to take out to extend the slide that began on Christmas Eve. The 200-day moving average is near $1.1580, and the euro has not traded below it since early March 2025.

• The dollar reached almost JPY159.20 near midday in NY yesterday and to JPY159.45 today. Japanese officials have climbed further the low rungs of the intervention escalation ladder, with new verbal warnings from the top Ministry of Finance officials, but it is hard to argue the market is disorderly or volatile. The actual (historic) one-week volatility is about 6.9%, while the one-month implied vol is about 8.2%, which is below the December high (~9.5%). Indeed, it is barely above the 50-day moving average (~8.5%). Last year’s low was near 7.2%. The greenback has been sold to around JPY158.60 in the European morning. Nearby support is seen closer to JPY158.20.

• Sterling was turned back yesterday in front of $1.35 and was sold to about $1.3425. It remained pinned near the lows through the North American afternoon. It has found support near $1.3420 today and has recovered back to $1.3460. The immediate risk is for some slippage back to around $1.3435.

• The greenback recovered from a three-day low around CAD1.3855 to approach CAD1.39 in a quiet and uneventful session yesterday. It is consolidating between CAD1.3880 and CAD1.3900 in quiet turnover today. The US dollar (DXY) has come a long way since the day after Christmas low (~CAD1.3645), but we suspect there is still scope for CAD1.3950-CAD1.4000.

• After rising to a four-day high slightly above $0.6725, the Australian dollar reversed lower and traded below Monday’s low (~$0.6675). It managed to settle slightly above it. The market seems to lack near-term conviction, and the Aussie is trading between about $0.6680 and slightly above $0.6700. It has approached session lows in the European morning, and our bias is on the upside in early North American activity.

EM

• While the dollar was mostly firmer yesterday, it was sold to a new low since July 2024 yesterday, slightly below MXN17.8150. It slipped below MXN17.81 today. Options of $820 mln expire tomorrow at MXN17.80. There seems little on the charts ahead of MXN17.60.

• Taking its cues from the dollar’s consolidation rather than Chinese trade figures, the greenback reached a three-day high against the offshore yuan today, slightly below CNH7.9785. The multiyear low was set on Monday near CNH6.9630. The PBOC set the dollar’s reference rate higher (CNY7.0120 vs. CNY7.0103 yesterday) for the first time in four sessions.

• The dollar was initially knocked down to INR89.98, a three-day low on sales by the Reserve Bank of India, but the buying interest proved too much, and the greenback recovered. It returned to yesterday’s high (~INR90.3015), the high since mid-December.

Other Markets

• Equities: The large bourse in Asia Pacific advanced, with the Nikkei’s nearly 1.5% rally leading the charge. China’s CSI 300 stumbled, as have India’s main indices. Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) is nursing a small gain, and US index futures are off 0.35%-0.65%

• Rates: The weak yen and prospects of a snap election keep the JGBs under pressure. The 10-year edged higher and, at 2.17%, is up 11 basis points since the end of last year. European benchmark yields are mostly a little lower, with the 10-year Gilt yield off nearly two basis points to extend this year’s decline almost 10 bp. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is nearly three basis points lower, to almost 4.15%, off 1.5 bp this year.

• Gold and silver at record highs. Gold reached about $4640, and silver traded above $91.50.

• March WTI’s surge continues. It traded below $56 last week and approached $62 today, its highest level since late September.

Data

• The US reports October and November PPI today, but the market remains highly convinced that the Federal Reserve is on hold, and even more so after the Justice Department action that has brought widespread condemnation. It is difficult to envisage any court taking the charges seriously. November retail sales by get a closer look. The median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey is for a 0.4% increase (same as October), though core retail sales may have slowed to 0.4% (from a heady 0.8% increase in October). The Q3 current account will also be reported. Economists seem to pay more attention to it than the market. The deficit is expected to narrow to about $239 bln, which would be the smallest since Q3 23. Portfolio inflows have easily financed it, with the TIC data showing foreign investors bought a net of more than $360 bln of US stocks and bonds in Q3. December existing home sales are also due. They likely rose for the fourth consecutive month but are still subdued. Five Fed officials speak today, including the uber-dove, Governor Miran. Lastly, it is another “decision day” for the Supreme Court. There is no pre-announcement of which cases have been decided, but it is possible that the president’s use of emergency powers to levy tariffs will be decided.

• The Polish central bank is expected to stand pat after cutting its reference rate by a quarter of a point in the last four meetings. It stands at 4.00%. The swap market is discounting 50 bp of cuts by mid-year. CPI was at 2.4% year-over-year in the initial estimate for December.

• China reported December trade figures. The surplus rose to $114.15 bln, which puts it at a whopping $1.19 trillion for the year. The 2024 surplus was about $992 bln. Exports rose 6.6% year-over-year in December, which is more than twice the increase the median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey anticipated. Imports were up 5.7%, compared with the 0.9% median forecast in Bloomberg’s survey. Despite a 20% drop in exports to the US, shipments rose 8.4% to Europe and 13.4% to Southeast Asia. Exports rose by over 25% to Africa. Note that some Chinese data suggests that around 28% of its exports are from “foreign-invested enterprises,” often a joint venture between a foreign firm and a Chinese company. About 20% of its auto exports are accounted for by foreign brands.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: December CPI Inflation: The Narrative Can Change In A Blink
data.description:
Nothing changed on the price front in December 2025, as both the headline and core inflation figures came in unchanged from the month before. Yet, the entire narrative surrounding these numbers has altered. Here's how.

When I checked on the inflation figures for November 2025, the drop, particularly in core inflation, was noteworthy. Coupled with the then recent weakness in the labor market, there was a good chance that the Fed could well sound dovish, if not cut rates outright in January 2026. It also looked like a good time to remain cautious.

Cut to now, and the picture looks very different after multiple data releases since, including the labor market report for December 2025, the GDP print for Q3 2025, and of course the latest inflation figures. Here, I discuss what the changing overall context means for monetary policy and stock market investments.

Headline CPI inflation stays steady...

First, a closer look at the inflation figures. At 2.7% YoY, headline inflation stands bang at the annual average. The number for Q4 2025 stands at 2.75%, assuming the unreleased figure for October due to the government shutdown came in at the average of the numbers for September and November, at 2.85%.

Going by historical trends, PCE inflation, which is the Fed's preferred inflation measure, will come in even lower. The reserve, however, projects a rise of 2.9% YoY for Q4 2025 for the PCE index, which is bigger than that for even the CPI. This indicates that PCE inflation remains firmly likely to undershoot the forecast, making for an argument for a rate cut later this month.

...as does core inflation

Similarly for core inflation. It's an even more heartening development that core inflation, which reflects more accurately on the longer-term inflation trajectory, also remained steady at 2.6% YoY.

Compared with the annual average of 2.9%, core inflation looks even better, indicating that prices are coming firmly under control. Also, with the Fed's target for Q4 2025 for core PCE inflation at 3%, it's now more than likely that this inflation figure, too, would be softer than forecast, as the core CPI inflation for Q4 2025 is at 2.7% YoY.

Exceptionally muted probability of a rate cut...

But the inflation release doesn't convince the markets that the reserve is ready to cut rates just yet. At the time of the previous inflation print, there was over a 25% probability of a rate cut. The figure had fallen dramatically to 4.4% following the recent employment report. It has dwindled even further to 2.8% following the CPI report.

This could have something to do with the headline inflation print coming slightly higher than the consensus expectation of 2.6% YoY. Also, the number might not indicate a rise in CPI inflation, but it doesn't indicate a continued falling off either. Seen in conjunction with the small drop in the unemployment rate in December 2025, it's not hard to see why markets just don't see monetary policy easing anytime soon.

...but could the Fed still be dovish?

It's entirely possible that the data itself for at least October and November 2025 could have inaccuracies due to the government shutdown, which would give the Fed another reason not to cut rates. Also, the Fed's own projections for economic growth in 2026 are strong.

At the same time, it's hard to overlook that PCE inflation is expected to come in softer than expected by the Fed in 2025. And this is despite the impact of a potential upward pressure from tariffs. Also, even with the latest drop in the unemployment report, labor market softening over the past year is evident. This suggests that the Fed can keep it tempered while with a bent towards the dovish side.

Markets influenced by earnings releases

The three key market indices were essentially indifferent, tending towards negative, at yesterday's close, with all of them seeing sub-1% drops. The Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) was most affected, with a 9.8% drop, while the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) and NASDAQ 100 Index (NDX) (QQQ) saw trivial softening (see Table 1 below).

While the market response is entirely in line with the unchanged CPI inflation figures, it's worth bearing in mind that the direct correlation between the indices and macroeconomic data and events can be muddled during earnings season. Yesterday, both JP Morgan (JPM) and Delta Air Lines (DAL) released their quarterly results. Both disappointed investors, with a 4.2% drop in JPM and a 2.2% correction in DAL.

The likely influence of these reports is evident from the fact that financial services saw the biggest correction among all sectors, of 2%, in yesterday's trading (see Table 2 below). It doesn't help that the CPI print doesn't provide any impetus for a rate cut later this month either.

Look towards cyclicals

A rate cut might not be coming this month, but monetary policy still stays in the easing cycle. This can be stimulating for an economy, which is expected to see a healthy performance in 2026 anyway.

This is one reason why I turned bullish about the S&P 500 for this year recently. Drilling down to the sectors, it should be no surprise that cyclicals are expected to be the likely gainers. In fact, despite the sector's latest wobble, financial services, as captured in the State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF), shows the most potential. Its forward P/E ratio showed the biggest undervaluation compared to its 5-year average levels (see section on "More sectors are undervalued than not" in the link).

Another sector to consider is materials, as the State Street Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB), which might have underperformed the S&P 500 over the past year, but on its own it didn't do too badly with a 14.4% increase. With a positive outlook on the economy, commodity prices could well inch up this year. In any case, the ETF is tempered by big holdings in gold mining stocks like Newmont Corporation (NEM).

In conclusion

The key takeaway here is that while the latest inflation print has remained unchanged, the entire narrative around it now lends to a starkly different story, as I was saying right at the start. Key data points released since show more strength in the economy than not, which can keep the Fed held back from rate changes right now. It is, however, a time to look more closely at cyclicals, which could see a good year ahead.

data.title: Zoetis: Cheap Valuation And Attractive Product Launch Pipeline
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Zoetis (ZTS) because of the upcoming product launches and a cheaper relative valuation vs. its closest peers. Once the near-term issues fade, which I expect them to eventually, the market should start to realize ZTS deserves a higher multiple.

Company description

ZTS develops, manufactures, and sells medicines and vaccines to veterinarians and livestock producers. It serves two main markets: (1) companion animals (dogs, cats, and horses) and (2) livestock (cattle, swine, poultry, and fish). ZTS has been making efforts to shift away from one-off products like vaccines toward chronic treatments for kidney disease, heart disease, and cancer.

Pipeline and CKD

This is the main anchor for ZTS’s bull case. Per management, ZTS has a pipeline of 12 potential blockbuster products over the next five years, with each blockbuster defined as >$100 million in annual sales. While there is no guarantee that all these products will work, I think it is encouraging that ZTS is targeting entirely new white space areas like oncology, cardiology, and renal disease, which has a combined total addressable market [TAM] of >$5 billion, and importantly, effective treatments barely exist today.

I think this strategy of targeting white spaces across several categories makes sense for three reasons. First, it structurally lowers risk. If all 12 pipeline assets were for the same condition, one failure could wipe out the entire growth story. By targeting different diseases, even if ZTS fails in one area, like cancer, it still has other opportunities. Second, because these are new markets, ZTS would not suffer from “cannibalism” by fighting for share with its own products in existing categories. Third, because there are not strong incumbent solutions, ZTS has a “low hurdle” to get over.

Of all the new markets, I believe the most important market in the near-term is renal disease, wherein ZTS has a potential breakthrough in chronic kidney disease [CKD]. In the Piper Sandler conference, management confirmed ZTS will be launching a first-in-class monoclonal antibody for canine renal disease in 2027, and I think this could be a game changer. For those that are unaware, CKD is one of the most common and serious conditions in pets, especially as they age, and yet treatment today is extremely basic (mainly just diet changes and some fluids). There is no effective drug that slows the disease progress or even cure it.

Just this market itself is worth ~$3 to $4 billion, so if ZTS’s drug can indeed solve this major challenge, it would have a very strong first-mover advantage that potentially allows it to monopolize this multi-billion-dollar market for years. This would be similar to Cytopoint in dermatology (a breakthrough injectable drug in veterinary dermatology made by ZTS), but potentially larger given how serious kidney disease is. Given there is a real risk of the pet dying when pets are diagnosed with kidney failure, owners are generally desperate for solutions and would likely pay any amount to save their precious one. To put it bluntly, this actually gives ZTS pricing power because there is really no low-cost alternatives.

Moreover, this CKD drug that ZTS propose is not a one-off fix. It is a recurring treatment (likely be given monthly for the rest of a pet’s life), and that gives ZTS a recurring stream of cash flow. The knock-on impact is that this would also drive up ZTS’s broader renal and diagnostics business, as vets would have a reason to test earlier and more often once a real treatment exists. Earlier testing leads to earlier diagnosis, which expands the number of pets eligible for treatment.

Competition in dermatology

A common bear case for ZTS is competition in the dermatology category, especially against its drugs like Cytopoint. I actually don’t think this is as much of as risk since ZTS already has a plan to launch a long-acting version of Cytopoint, which is expected to receive US approval in H2 2026 (per the Piper Sandler conference). Instead of injections every 4 to 8 weeks, this upgraded Cytopoint is designed to control itching for several months with a single dose. The implication here is that fewer injections mean fewer vet visits, and better convenience for both vets and pet owners.

You might ask why would vets prefer lesser visits, it is because the industry is currently facing a massive labor shortage, meaning to say capacity, and not demand, is the bottleneck. For short-term recurring routine injections that carry little margin, it means a timeslot wasted that can be used for a high-margin surgery. Essentially, the new Cytopoint solves this “yield problem” for vets. Also, for the vets, inventory management becomes a lot simpler since they don’t need to carry so much stock keeping units, which reduces storage needs and the risk of expired products (100% loss in this case).

Given that vets are already well aware of Cytopoint (pros, cons, and how it works), there shouldn’t be any major friction in getting then to adopt the new Cytopoint.

Valuation

I believe ZTS’s valuation is the biggest disconnect in the equity story. The stock trade at just 18.5x NTM PE vs. its historical average of 29x, and the way I see it, this discount is really driven by near-term non-structural issues. Specifically, the Librela deceleration and softer veterinary visit trends. Regarding Librela, the recent softness was driven primarily by "misperceptions" regarding safety that were amplified on social media. Essentially this is a panic that is not driven by a fundamental failure of the drug's efficacy. ZTS is already addressing this with educational campaigns, and we are already seeing signs of stabilization. As the social media narrative fades and the clinical data prove the drug’s efficacy, growth should recover.

As for the softness in veterinary visits, this is really a cyclical dynamic, not a structural one. High inflation and poor macro have caused consumers to tighten their belts, but if we take a step back and look at the long-term, the secular trend of "pet humanization" remains intact. More importantly, investors seem to be focusing on the wrong metric. With the shift toward long-term injectables (like the upcoming Cytopoint LA), the correlation between visit volume and revenue growth is going to get wider since a vet only needs to administer one injection that lasts multiple months. In other words, this metric is not going to be the best indicator for growth.

To give a better sense of things, consider that Elanco Animal Healthcare (ELAN) trades at ~23x NTM PE with the exact same revenue growth outlook as ZTS (mid-single-digits), but is half the revenue size, has lower adj. EBITDA margin (~10pts difference), and a much higher debt profile (~4x net-debt to EBITDA vs. ZTS at 1.3x). If anything, ZTS should trade at a premium when we compare side by side. I believe the catalyst for this re-rating is when the new drugs in the pipeline comes online. If that happens and multiple goes back to at least 23x, this is an easy 24% upside.

Risk

If pipeline launches are delayed, fail, or see slow adoption, growth could disappoint. Competition in dermatology is not something we can completely dismiss either. If there is a new groundbreaking drug, it could disrupt ZTS market position and hurt both growth and margin.

Conclusion

I am positive on ZTS because of its strong pipeline, defensible position in the dermatology category, and also because it is cheap on a relative valuation basis. I believe the market is too focused on short-term issues and is underestimating the impact of the upcoming launches and the Cytopoint upgrade.
